definition: The methodological principle that with n=3-4 fiscal dominance episodes, n=7 resolution pathways, and n=0 for the current US configuration, all historically-extrapolated claims should carry maximum confidence of 5-6/10. Claims grounded in current market observables or accounting identities may reach 7-8/10. The Clopper-Pearson interval on '50% repression probability' from 4/7 episodes spans [15%, 85%].
evidence: n=3-4 for fiscal dominance episodes; n=7 for resolution pathways; n=0 for current US configuration
Clopper-Pearson 95% CI on 50% repression probability spans [15%, 85%]
Pair_3 debate reached nuanced resolution on differentiated confidence caps
Airtight logical deduction: if small-n deserves confidence 9, no downstream extrapolative claim should exceed 5-6/10
open_questions: How to formalize the taxonomy of claim types (historical extrapolation vs contemporary observation vs accounting identity)?
Should the cap be applied to scenario probabilities or mechanism descriptions?
Can Bayesian priors from related but non-identical episodes partially mitigate the small-n problem?
